| **phase_prompt** | Current process text: {reviewed_process_text} Extra knowledge: 1) The activity 'Provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies...' should be the last activity after everything else is done and before the end activity. 2) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be prioritized as the initial steps in their respective flow or branch as they are prerequisites for other activities. 3) All tests, including any type of imaging and blood tests, should be immediately interpreted (followed) by a healthcare expert. 4) Blood tests or any tests that take some time to get results should occur concurrently with other activities to allow faster treatment unless the test results are required for a specific task, in which case they will be merged into the flow or branch of that activity as they are now prerequisites. 5) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be put in a single activity. 6) Assessing airway, breathing, and circulation is called ABC testing and is ONE activity not multiple activities. 7) Neurological examination and imaging results are both needed (prerequisites) to Evaluate patients for endovascular therapy and should happen before this. However, they can be in parallel to other activities of the pathway that don’t require these results to allow efficient time management. 8) CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, glucose, INR, partial thromboplastin time, and troponin test are all types of blood tests, and they all should be merged in ONE blood works activity if they are at happening in the same place of the process. 9) Anti platelet therapy has 4 general options (ASA, Clopidogrel, Extended-release Dipyridamole + Aspirin (Aggrenox), and rectal or gastric ASA if can't swallow) and they always should be indicated right after each initiation of antiplatelet therapy activity in the process. 10) Different Temperature reducing activities (options) should happen concurrently (in regard to each other) to reduce the the temperature as fast as possible and continue care. 11) The activity "chest x-ray should not delay..." means IF chest x-ray is required it should not delay assessment and IF not required it should be skipped and continue. 12) If the evaluation in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital is needed, it should happen in parallel (concurrent) to everything else and not in sequence. 13) When patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression in the same part of the process, they should be merged into ONE activity along with forwarding the results. 14) Assessments related and needed in order to formulate a management plan should be merged into one activity along with formulating the management plan. 15) Formulating the management plan is a prerequisite for all subsequent activities and assessments. 16) Mobilization should be considered only if clinical judgment supports it within the first 24 hours of stroke onset; between 24–48 hours, proceed with mobilization if no contraindications are identified, otherwise skip and continue with the care pathway. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure Module 1: Risk Stratification Module 1 Recommended Practices 1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals. 1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis Initial investigations should include: • serum creatinine and electrolyte levels • troponin measurements • complete blood count • electrocardiogram • chest x-ray examination • an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available • frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized 1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED. Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing. High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing. High-risk markers include: • respiratory distress • hypoxemia • severity of pulmonary edema • poor response to furosemide administered in ED • hemodynamic compromise • significant arrhythmias • positive troponin 1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score). As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital. [2026-02-03 20:06:27 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a Knowledge Graph Advisor. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your job is to review process models and provide actionable suggestions for improvement, one at a time.
You use extra knowledge provided to you to inform your suggestions. Your main responsibilities include giving suggestions and your reasoning to the process design expert.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.]

Current process text:
<process>
  <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='develop and consistently apply risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF)' objects='' id='activity1'/>

        </branch>

          <activity role='clinical staff' action='frequently measure oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized' objects='' id='activity9.3'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

    <branch id='branch10'>

              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess respiratory distress' objects='' id='activity11.1'/>
            </branch>

            </branch>

              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess poor response to furosemide administered in ED' objects='' id='activity11.4'/>
            </branch>

              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess hemodynamic compromise' objects='' id='activity11.5'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch36'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess significant arrhythmias' objects='' id='activity11.6'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch37'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess positive troponin' objects='' id='activity11.7'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch39'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='calculate an acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk' objects='' id='activity12'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch11'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess risk-stratification patient group (low intensity / average intensity / high intensity)' objects='' id='activity10'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='treat in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharge home without requiring inpatient admission' objects='' id='activity16'/>
      <activity role='primary care provider' action='conduct a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='admit to hospital' objects='' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>

  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway3'>
    <branch id='branch21'>
      <activity role='hospitals' action='use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department' objects='' id='activity21'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch22'>
      <activity role='hospitals' action='have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider' objects='' id='activity22'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
</process>
Extra knowledge:
1) The activity 'Provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies...' should be the last activity after everything else is done and before the end activity.
2) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be prioritized as the initial steps in their respective flow or branch as they are prerequisites for other activities.
3) All tests, including any type of imaging and blood tests, should be immediately interpreted (followed) by a healthcare expert.
4) Blood tests or any tests that take some time to get results should occur concurrently with other activities to allow faster treatment unless the test results are required for a specific task, in which case they will be merged into the flow or branch of that activity as they are now prerequisites.
5) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be put in a single activity.
6) Assessing airway, breathing, and circulation is called ABC testing and is ONE activity not multiple activities.
7) Neurological examination and imaging results are both needed (prerequisites) to Evaluate patients for endovascular therapy and should happen before this. However, they can be in parallel to other activities of the pathway that don’t require these results to allow efficient time management.
8) CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, glucose, INR, partial thromboplastin time, and troponin test are all types of blood tests, and they all should be merged in ONE blood works activity if they are at happening in the same place of the process.
9) Anti platelet therapy has 4 general options (ASA, Clopidogrel, Extended-release Dipyridamole + Aspirin (Aggrenox), and rectal or gastric ASA if can't swallow) and they always should be indicated right after each initiation of antiplatelet therapy activity in the process.
10) Different Temperature reducing activities (options) should happen concurrently (in regard to each other) to reduce the the temperature as fast as possible and continue care.
11) The activity "chest x-ray should not delay..." means IF chest x-ray is required it should not delay assessment and IF not required it should be skipped and continue.
12) If the evaluation in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital is needed, it should happen in parallel (concurrent) to everything else and not in sequence.
13) When patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression in the same part of the process, they should be merged into ONE activity along with forwarding the results.
14) Assessments related and needed in order to formulate a management plan should be merged into one activity along with formulating the management plan.
15) Formulating the management plan is a prerequisite for all subsequent activities and assessments.
16) Mobilization should be considered only if clinical judgment supports it within the first 24 hours of stroke onset; between 24–48 hours, proceed with mobilization if no contraindications are identified, otherwise skip and continue with the care pathway.

<end of extra knowledge>

When making recommendations, don't forget the format of the process text:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.


Format your suggestion in the following format:
Suggested modification: <your suggestion>
Reason: <your reasoning>

Based on the above extra knowledge, provide ONE actionable suggestion along with your reasoning to improve the process. If there are no further suggestions, just reply with: 'There are no further suggestions.'

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital. Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence. Module 1 Implementation Considerations General Considerations for Risk Stratification • Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department. • All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider |

[2026-02-03 20:06:30 INFO] Knowledge Graph Advisor: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.
Use ONLY the provided QBP text as the source of truth. Do NOT invent tasks, roles, objects, or documentation steps unless explicitly stated.
Do NOT add 'confirmation/verification/documenting/sending results' tasks unless the text explicitly says so.
Represent conditions as gateway branch conditions, not as activities.
If the text does not specify order between activities, model them as parallel.
Granularity rule: apply the same bullet-handling policy consistently across the whole model (see phase rules).]

Task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider

    <branch id='branch1'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway4'>
        <branch id='branch23'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='measure serum creatinine' objects='' id='activity2.1'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch24'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='measure electrolyte levels' objects='' id='activity2.2'/>
        </branch>

    </branch>

    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform chest x-ray examination' objects='' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch6'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch7'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform an echocardiogram' objects='' id='activity7'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='a recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='do not perform an echocardiogram' objects='' id='activity8'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch9'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway5'>

        </branch>

  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='treat in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharge home without requiring inpatient admission' objects='' id='activity16'/>
      <activity role='primary care provider' action='conduct a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='admit to hospital' objects='' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>

  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway3'>
    <branch id='branch21'>
      <activity role='hospitals' action='use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department' objects='' id='activity21'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch22'>
      <activity role='hospitals' action='have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider' objects='' id='activity22'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
</process>
Advisor's suggestion:
Suggested modification:
<process>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway3_mod'>
    <branch id='branch21_mod'>
      <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='implement a common standardized risk-stratification tool/process and a pathway to transfer patients to a higher level provider' objects='' id='activity_hospitals_prereq_mod'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch22_mod'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='develop and consistently apply risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF)' objects='' id='activity1'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
</process>

Reason: Hospital-level prerequisites (standardized risk stratification and transfer/escalation mechanism) should be prioritized as initial steps and combined into a single activity (extra knowledge #2 and #5), because they enable and standardize downstream ED risk stratification and disposition decisions.
You need to modify the process text according to the suggestions and output it.
Be careful not to change the format:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

[2026-02-03 20:06:43 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Knowledge Graph Advisor on : ProcessDesignerFix, turn 0**

[You're a Knowledge Graph Advisor. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your job is to review process models and provide actionable suggestions for improvement, one at a time.
You use extra knowledge provided to you to inform your suggestions. Your main responsibilities include giving suggestions and your reasoning to the process design expert.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.]

| **phase_prompt** | Current process text: {reviewed_process_text} Extra knowledge: 1) The activity 'Provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies...' should be the last activity after everything else is done and before the end activity. 2) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be prioritized as the initial steps in their respective flow or branch as they are prerequisites for other activities. 3) All tests, including any type of imaging and blood tests, should be immediately interpreted (followed) by a healthcare expert. 4) Blood tests or any tests that take some time to get results should occur concurrently with other activities to allow faster treatment unless the test results are required for a specific task, in which case they will be merged into the flow or branch of that activity as they are now prerequisites. 5) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be put in a single activity. 6) Assessing airway, breathing, and circulation is called ABC testing and is ONE activity not multiple activities. 7) Neurological examination and imaging results are both needed (prerequisites) to Evaluate patients for endovascular therapy and should happen before this. However, they can be in parallel to other activities of the pathway that don’t require these results to allow efficient time management. 8) CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, glucose, INR, partial thromboplastin time, and troponin test are all types of blood tests, and they all should be merged in ONE blood works activity if they are at happening in the same place of the process. 9) Anti platelet therapy has 4 general options (ASA, Clopidogrel, Extended-release Dipyridamole + Aspirin (Aggrenox), and rectal or gastric ASA if can't swallow) and they always should be indicated right after each initiation of antiplatelet therapy activity in the process. 10) Different Temperature reducing activities (options) should happen concurrently (in regard to each other) to reduce the the temperature as fast as possible and continue care. 11) The activity "chest x-ray should not delay..." means IF chest x-ray is required it should not delay assessment and IF not required it should be skipped and continue. 12) If the evaluation in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital is needed, it should happen in parallel (concurrent) to everything else and not in sequence. 13) When patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression in the same part of the process, they should be merged into ONE activity along with forwarding the results. 14) Assessments related and needed in order to formulate a management plan should be merged into one activity along with formulating the management plan. 15) Formulating the management plan is a prerequisite for all subsequent activities and assessments. 16) Mobilization should be considered only if clinical judgment supports it within the first 24 hours of stroke onset; between 24–48 hours, proceed with mobilization if no contraindications are identified, otherwise skip and continue with the care pathway. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure Module 1: Risk Stratification Module 1 Recommended Practices 1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals. 1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis Initial investigations should include: • serum creatinine and electrolyte levels • troponin measurements • complete blood count • electrocardiogram • chest x-ray examination • an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available • frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized 1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. [2026-02-03 20:06:43 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a Knowledge Graph Advisor. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your job is to review process models and provide actionable suggestions for improvement, one at a time.
You use extra knowledge provided to you to inform your suggestions. Your main responsibilities include giving suggestions and your reasoning to the process design expert.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.]

Current process text:
<process>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway3_mod'>
    <branch id='branch21_mod'>
      <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='implement a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department AND have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider' objects='' id='activity_hospitals_prereq_mod'/>
    </branch>

        </branch>
        <branch condition='a recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='do not perform an echocardiogram' objects='' id='activity8'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch9'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway5'>
        <branch id='branch25'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='frequently measure heart rate until patient is stabilized' objects='' id='activity9.1'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch26'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='frequently measure blood pressure until patient is stabilized' objects='' id='activity9.2'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch27'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='frequently measure oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized' objects='' id='activity9.3'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
    <branch id='branch10'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway8'>
        <branch id='branch38'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway7'>
            <branch id='branch31'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess respiratory distress' objects='' id='activity11.1'/>
            </branch>

              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess hypoxemia' objects='' id='activity11.2'/>

            </branch>

              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess poor response to furosemide administered in ED' objects='' id='activity11.4'/>
            </branch>

              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess hemodynamic compromise' objects='' id='activity11.5'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch36'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess significant arrhythmias' objects='' id='activity11.6'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch37'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess positive troponin' objects='' id='activity11.7'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch39'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='calculate an acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk' objects='' id='activity12'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch11'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess risk-stratification patient group (low intensity / average intensity / high intensity)' objects='' id='activity10'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='treat in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharge home without requiring inpatient admission' objects='' id='activity16'/>
      <activity role='primary care provider' action='conduct a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='admit to hospital' objects='' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
Extra knowledge:
1) The activity 'Provide cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies...' should be the last activity after everything else is done and before the end activity.
2) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be prioritized as the initial steps in their respective flow or branch as they are prerequisites for other activities.
3) All tests, including any type of imaging and blood tests, should be immediately interpreted (followed) by a healthcare expert.
4) Blood tests or any tests that take some time to get results should occur concurrently with other activities to allow faster treatment unless the test results are required for a specific task, in which case they will be merged into the flow or branch of that activity as they are now prerequisites.
5) Activities related to stroke units, staffing, or hospitals in the QBP should be put in a single activity.
6) Assessing airway, breathing, and circulation is called ABC testing and is ONE activity not multiple activities.
7) Neurological examination and imaging results are both needed (prerequisites) to Evaluate patients for endovascular therapy and should happen before this. However, they can be in parallel to other activities of the pathway that don’t require these results to allow efficient time management.
8) CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, glucose, INR, partial thromboplastin time, and troponin test are all types of blood tests, and they all should be merged in ONE blood works activity if they are at happening in the same place of the process.
9) Anti platelet therapy has 4 general options (ASA, Clopidogrel, Extended-release Dipyridamole + Aspirin (Aggrenox), and rectal or gastric ASA if can't swallow) and they always should be indicated right after each initiation of antiplatelet therapy activity in the process.
10) Different Temperature reducing activities (options) should happen concurrently (in regard to each other) to reduce the the temperature as fast as possible and continue care.
11) The activity "chest x-ray should not delay..." means IF chest x-ray is required it should not delay assessment and IF not required it should be skipped and continue.
12) If the evaluation in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital is needed, it should happen in parallel (concurrent) to everything else and not in sequence.
13) When patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression in the same part of the process, they should be merged into ONE activity along with forwarding the results.
14) Assessments related and needed in order to formulate a management plan should be merged into one activity along with formulating the management plan.
15) Formulating the management plan is a prerequisite for all subsequent activities and assessments.
16) Mobilization should be considered only if clinical judgment supports it within the first 24 hours of stroke onset; between 24–48 hours, proceed with mobilization if no contraindications are identified, otherwise skip and continue with the care pathway.

<end of extra knowledge>

When making recommendations, don't forget the format of the process text:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.


Format your suggestion in the following format:
Suggested modification: <your suggestion>
Reason: <your reasoning>

Based on the above extra knowledge, provide ONE actionable suggestion along with your reasoning to improve the process. If there are no further suggestions, just reply with: 'There are no further suggestions.'

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.

Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

Suggested modification:
```xml
<process>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway3_mod'>
    <branch id='branch21_mod'>
      <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='implement a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department AND have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider' objects='' id='activity_hospitals_prereq_mod'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch22_mod'>
      <activity role='Ontario hospitals' action='develop and consistently apply risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF)' objects='' id='activity1'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='order and collect bloodwork (serum creatinine, electrolytes, troponin, complete blood count)' objects='' id='activity_bloodwork_merged'/>
      <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret bloodwork results' objects='creatinine, electrolytes, troponin, CBC results' id='activity_interpret_bloodwork'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform electrocardiogram' objects='' id='activity5'/>
      <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret electrocardiogram' objects='ECG results' id='activity_interpret_ecg'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch5'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform chest x-ray examination' objects='' id='activity6'/>
      <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret chest x-ray' objects='chest x-ray results' id='activity_interpret_cxr'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch6'>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='no recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch7'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='perform an echocardiogram' objects='' id='activity7'/>
          <activity role='healthcare expert' action='interpret echocardiogram' objects='echocardiogram results' id='activity_interpret_echo'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='a recent echocardiogram is available' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='retrieve recent echocardiogram report' objects='recent echocardiogram results' id='activity_retrieve_echo'/>
          <activity role='healthcare expert' action='review recent echocardiogram' objects='recent echocardiogram results' id='activity_review_echo'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch9'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway5'>
        <branch id='branch25'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='frequently measure heart rate until patient is stabilized' objects='' id='activity9.1'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch26'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='frequently measure blood pressure until patient is stabilized' objects='' id='activity9.2'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch27'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='frequently measure oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized' objects='' id='activity9.3'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
    <branch id='branch10'>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway8'>
        <branch id='branch38'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway7'>
            <branch id='branch31'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess respiratory distress' objects='' id='activity11.1'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch32'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess hypoxemia' objects='' id='activity11.2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch33'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess severity of pulmonary edema' objects='' id='activity11.3'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch34'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess poor response to furosemide administered in ED' objects='' id='activity11.4'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch35'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess hemodynamic compromise' objects='' id='activity11.5'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch36'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess significant arrhythmias' objects='' id='activity11.6'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch37'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess positive troponin' objects='' id='activity11.7'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch39'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='calculate an acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk' objects='' id='activity12'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch11'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess risk-stratification patient group (low intensity / average intensity / high intensity)' objects='' id='activity10'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='treat in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharge home without requiring inpatient admission' objects='' id='activity16'/>
      <activity role='primary care provider' action='conduct a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='admit to hospital' objects='' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
```

Reason: This (1) merges creatinine/electrolytes/troponin/CBC into one concurrent bloodwork activity (more realistic ordering/collection), and (2) adds immediate expert interpretation after each test/imaging (bloodwork, ECG, CXR, echo), aligning the model with the requirement that all tests be followed by interpretation to enable timely clinical decisions.

| **task_prompt** | Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above). Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure Module 1: Risk Stratification Module 1 Recommended Practices 1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals. 1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis Initial investigations should include: • serum creatinine and electrolyte levels • troponin measurements • complete blood count • electrocardiogram • chest x-ray examination • an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available • frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized 1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED. Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing. High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing. High-risk markers include: • respiratory distress • hypoxemia • severity of pulmonary edema • poor response to furosemide administered in ED • hemodynamic compromise • significant arrhythmias • positive troponin 1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score). As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital. As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital. [2026-02-03 20:07:01 INFO] Knowledge Graph Advisor: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider.
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.
Use ONLY the provided QBP text as the source of truth. Do NOT invent tasks, roles, objects, or documentation steps unless explicitly stated.
Do NOT add 'confirmation/verification/documenting/sending results' tasks unless the text explicitly says so.
Represent conditions as gateway branch conditions, not as activities.
If the text does not specify order between activities, model them as parallel.
Granularity rule: apply the same bullet-handling policy consistently across the whole model (see phase rules).]

Task: Episode of Care for Acute Heart Failure

The Acute Heart Failure Episode-of-Care Advisory Panel developed the episode-of-care model for acute heart failure (Figure 6). Modules 1 through 3 represent the acute heart failure episode of care. The following recommendations include the recommendations from the clinical handbook on acute heart failure published in 2013 and updates to the recommendations (as noted in the What’s New box above).

Figure 6. Episode-of-Care Model for Acute Heart Failure

Module 1: Risk Stratification

Module 1 Recommended Practices

1.1 Risk Assessment/Stratification
Risk-stratification tools that can be used for multiple conditions (including HF) should be developed and consistently applied across all Ontario hospitals.

1.2 Responsiveness to Diuresis
Initial investigations should include:
• serum creatinine and electrolyte levels
• troponin measurements
• complete blood count
• electrocardiogram
• chest x-ray examination
• an echocardiogram if no recent echocardiogram is available
• frequent measurement of heart rate, blood pressure, and oxygen saturation until patient is stabilized

1.3 Risk Stratification Patient Groups
Low intensity: Patients can be treated in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharged home without requiring inpatient admission. However, these patients require a follow-up visit with their primary care provider within days of discharge from the ED.
Average intensity: Patients require admission to inpatient care with normal nurse-to-patient staffing.
High intensity: Patients require ventilation (either noninvasive or invasive ventilation) and/or admission to an intensive care unit with higher nurse-to-patient staffing.

High-risk markers include:
• respiratory distress
• hypoxemia
• severity of pulmonary edema
• poor response to furosemide administered in ED
• hemodynamic compromise
• significant arrhythmias
• positive troponin

1.4 Heart Failure Risk Score
An acute heart failure risk score—for example, the EHMRG—be calculated to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk of HF patients (predicted mortality risk increases incrementally with higher EHMRG risk score).

As a general guide, patients who are low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) can be considered for discharge home if they have responded to initial treatment in the ED, provided that there are no other considerations (e.g., advanced-directives, severe dementia, estimated impact of admission on life-expectancy, etc.). Patients who are at higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5) should be admitted to hospital.

Abbreviations: ACCF, American College of Cardiology Foundation; AHA, American Heart Association; CCS, Canadian Cardiovascular Society; ED, emergency department; EHMRG, Emergency Heart Failure Mortality Risk Grade; HF, heart failure; NICE, National Institute for Clinical Excellence.

Module 1 Implementation Considerations
General Considerations for Risk Stratification
• Hospitals should use a common standardized risk stratification assessment tool or process to determine where and how to assist with clinical decision making when patients present to the emergency department.
• All hospitals should have a pathway or mechanism to transfer patients to a higher level provider

            <branch id='branch33'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess severity of pulmonary edema' objects='' id='activity11.3'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch34'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess poor response to furosemide administered in ED' objects='' id='activity11.4'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch35'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess hemodynamic compromise' objects='' id='activity11.5'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch36'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess significant arrhythmias' objects='' id='activity11.6'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch37'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess positive troponin' objects='' id='activity11.7'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch39'>
          <activity role='clinical staff' action='calculate an acute heart failure risk score (e.g., EHMRG) to assist with clinical decision-making and predicting the 7-day mortality risk' objects='' id='activity12'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch11'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess risk-stratification patient group (low intensity / average intensity / high intensity)' objects='' id='activity10'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>

  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
    <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations' id='branch13'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='treat in the ED or in outpatient settings and discharge home without requiring inpatient admission' objects='' id='activity16'/>
      <activity role='primary care provider' action='conduct a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='admit to hospital' objects='' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
</process>
Advisor's suggestion:

      <activity role='clinical staff' action='assess risk-stratification patient group (low intensity / average intensity / high intensity)' objects='' id='activity10'/>

    <branch condition='low-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 1 and 2) AND responded to initial treatment in the ED AND no other considerations' id='branch13'>

      <activity role='primary care provider' action='conduct a follow-up visit within days of discharge from the ED' objects='' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='higher-risk (e.g., EHMRG quintiles 3-5)' id='branch14'>
      <activity role='clinical staff' action='admit to hospital' objects='' id='activity15'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
